cards.back: ℒ{sin} = ω/(s²+ω²), poles at ±jω. ℒ{cos} = s/(s²+ω²), same poles.

cards.front: What happens to the transient part?
cards.back: K₁e^(−t/τ) decays to zero after ~5τ. Only the steady-state sinusoid remains.

cards.front: Where does the transient come from?
cards.back: System poles (e.g. s=−1/RC). Where does SS come from? Input poles (s=±jω).
|H(jω)| = 1/√(1+(ωRC)²). Maximum at ω=0 (|H|=1). Decreases with frequency → low-pass.
∠H = −arctan(ωRC). Always negative → output lags input. At ω=1/RC: phase = −45°.

cards.front: Complex conjugate PFE terms combine how?
Why does LTI preserve input frequency?
cards.back: Linearity: no new frequencies created. Time invariance: no mixing. Output sinusoid has same ω, just scaled and shifted.
y(∞) via FVT = 0. This is the average value of the sinusoid. There's no 'final' value — the signal oscillates forever.

cards.front: At ω=1/RC, what is the output?
cards.back: Amplitude ×1/√2 ≈ 70.7%, phase = −45°. This is the −3dB cutoff point of the RC filter.
y(t) = K₁e^(−t/τ) + |H(jω)|·A·sin(ωt+∠H(jω)). Transient from system pole, SS from input poles.
